steps.title: Use a one unit multiplier
steps.content: Let $k=b^{1/n}$. Then $f(x)=a\cdot k^x$, so $k$ is the multiplier for each increase of $1$ in $x$.

steps.title: Find the multiplier
steps.content:
From $x=3$ to $x=5$, the input increases by $2$:
$$
\frac{f(5)}{f(3)}=\frac{112}{7}=16
$$
So $k^2=16$, and because $k$ is positive, $k=4$.

steps.title: Move one more input unit
steps.content: From $x=5$ to $x=6$, multiply once by $4$: $$ f(6)=112\cdot 4=448 $$ The answer is 448.
